summary:
The Laurentide Ice Sheet was the massive continental glacier that covered most of Canada and parts of the northern United States during the last glacial maximum, reaching its greatest extent around 26,500–20,000 BCE when it covered roughly 13 million square kilometers of North America to depths of up to 3–4 kilometers. The retreat of the Laurentide Ice Sheet following the [[event:1262|end of the last Ice Age]] was one of the most consequential environmental events in North American prehistory, opening vast territories to human colonization and fundamentally reshaping the continent's hydrology, climate, and ecology.

Deglaciation of the Laurentide proceeded in pulses driven by warming temperatures, with the southern margins retreating from approximately 19,000 BCE onward. The retreat opened an ice-free corridor along the eastern flank of the Rocky Mountains that may have served as one route for the southward dispersal of humans from Beringia into the Americas, though debate continues about whether this corridor was biologically productive enough to support human passage before approximately 12,000 BCE. By 10,000–8,000 BCE, much of the Great Plains and the Great Lakes region had been deglaciated, though substantial ice remained over Hudson Bay, Quebec, and the Canadian Maritimes.

The melting of the Laurentide contributed enormously to global sea level rise — ultimately adding approximately 60–70 meters to global ocean levels. The enormous meltwater lakes that formed at the margins of the retreating ice — including glacial Lake Agassiz, the largest freshwater lake in North American history — periodically drained catastrophically into the North Atlantic, contributing to the disruptions of the [[event:1261|Younger Dryas]] and other climatic events. The retreat of the Laurentide created the modern Great Lakes, the St. Lawrence River, Hudson Bay, and the landscape of central and eastern Canada — a radical transformation of North American geography in the span of a few thousand years.
